coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the overuse of antibiotics has caused more and more serious environmental pollution, the uncontrolled abuse of antibiotics makes bacteria produce resistance to antibiotics faster than the replacement rate of antibiotics themselves, leading to the emergence of super drug-resistant bacteria. Therefore, it is of great practical significance to establish a simple, rapid and sensitive method for the detection of antibiotics. By integrating natural nano-clay (Atta) and carbon dots (CDs), the real-time and rapid visual detection of tetracycline (TC) in the sample can be realized by chromaticity pick-up APP on smartphone. The nano-sensor can detect tetracycline in the concentration between 25 nM and 20 μM with the detection limit of 8.7 nM. The low detection limit coupled with good accuracy, sensitivity and specificity meets the requirements for the detection of tetracycline in food. More importantly, the test paper and fluorescent stick-like nano-sensor are designed to detect tetracycline by polychromatic fluorescence changes. In addition, a logic gate for semi-quantitative identification of the concentration of tetracycline is designed, which makes it possible for the application of the nano-sensor in the field of smart devices.

head.ce:title: A stick-like intelligent multicolor nano-sensor for the detection of tetracycline: The integration of nano-clay and carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, the overuse of antibiotics has caused more and more serious environmental pollution, the uncontrolled abuse of antibiotics makes bacteria produce resistance to antibiotics faster than the replacement rate of antibiotics themselves, leading to the emergence of super drug-resistant bacteria. Therefore, it is of great practical significance to establish a simple, rapid and sensitive method for the detection of antibiotics. By integrating natural nano-clay (Atta) and carbon dots (CDs), the real-time and rapid visual detection of tetracycline (TC) in the sample can be realized by chromaticity pick-up APP on smartphone. The nano-sensor can detect tetracycline in the concentration between 25 nM and 20 μM with the detection limit of 8.7 nM. The low detection limit coupled with good accuracy, sensitivity and specificity meets the requirements for the detection of tetracycline in food. More importantly, the test paper and fluorescent stick-like nano-sensor are designed to detect tetracycline by polychromatic fluorescence changes. In addition, a logic gate for semi-quantitative identification of the concentration of tetracycline is designed, which makes it possible for the application of the nano-sensor in the field of smart devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the invention of antibiotics, diseases related to bacterial infections have been controlled effectively. Among them, tetracycline antibiotics, as a kind of multifunctional broad-spectrum antibiotics, are widely used to heal bacterial infections in animals and humans because they can inhibit the protein synthesis of bacteria (Feng et al., 2015; Xiong et al., 2019). Furthermore, tetracycline antibiotics can promote the growth of animals, therefore are used as a feed additive in animal husbandry (Ben et al., 2019). Unfortunately, excessive abuse of antibiotics has had a series of extremely serious consequences. In general, TC can be degraded by most biodegradable (Mahamallik et al., 2015), so the residual TC may lead to selective genetic variation of microorganisms, leading to the emergence of drug-resistant pathogens. Bacteria can also obtain antibiotic resistance genes through gene mutation or gene transfer (Zhou et al., 2017). The exchange mechanism of antibiotic resistance genes between agricultural soil bacteria and clinical pathogens often leads to the spread of antibiotic resistance in a certain range (Forsberg et al., 2012), leading to the emergence of "superbugs". Recently research has shown that more than 30 antibiotics that include a variety of tetracyclines and quinolones have been detected in karst river system in Kaiyang, southwest China (Katz et al., 1998; Drew et al., 1999; Ford and Williams, 2007; Heinz et al., 2009; Huang et al., 2019), which can pose a threat to non-target organisms of different trophic levels including bacteria, algae, plants, invertebrates, and fish (Danner et al., 2019; Felis et al., 2020). Significant ecological risks (e.g., inhibition of algae and cyanobacteria growth) of many kinds of antibiotics have been widely reported elsewhere in China (Liu et al., 2018), South Korea (Kim et al., 2019), Malaysia (Low et al., 2020), and Vietnam (Ngo et al., 2020). At the same time, the worldwide abuse of antibiotics leads to the widespread spread and transfer of resistant microorganisms between humans and animals, which greatly endangers the health and safety of human beings (He et al., 2019; Nataliya et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020). Therefore, from the point of view of clinical needs or the responsibilities of animal detection departments and even food safety departments, the detection of tetracycline residues is very important.

The traditional detection methods of tetracycline mainly comprise liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Moreno-Gonzalez and García-Campaña, 2017), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Al-Afy et al., 2018) and capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Moreno-González et al., 2017). Although LC-MS and HPLC have the advantages of high sensitivity and good accuracy, the requirement of expensive instruments and professional operators limit their application in on-site rapid testing (Lan et al., 2017). CE has some disadvantages, such as time-consuming, tedious, large amount of reagent, low separation efficiency and so on (Ibarra et al., 2011). Other techniques such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) (Jiang et al., 2013), thin-layer chromatography (TLC) (Chen and Schwack, 2014), electrochemical method (Chen et al., 2017) and other method also are successfully used to detect TC with highly accuracy and sensitivity in recently years. However, these new techniques also have their inherent shortcomings. For example, ELISA is specific, but the antibody reagent is expensive and difficult to be preserved, and the spectrophotometric analysis is easy to produce false positive due to the interference of the color of the background matrix (Chen et al., 2020). Therefore, many researchers are committed to developing more convenient and effective methods for the detection of antibiotics.

Compared with these methods, fluorescence method is considered as an effective tetracycline detection method because of its convenience, low cost, high sensitivity, fast response and easy visual analysis (Li et al., 2020). The classical fluorescence detection and analysis methods usually can only detect the target by measuring the change of a single fluorescence signal, and it is often interfered by a variety of irrelevant factors to reduce the sensitivity and selectivity (Chen et al., 2020). The ratio fluorescence detection method can realize the analysis of the object to be measured by measuring the relative change intensity of different fluorescence signals, which can effectively reduce a variety of interference and improve the degree of visualization (Gao et al., 2018). At present, most of the ratio fluorescent sensors use one or two of the traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (Kim et al., 2013; Chao et al., 2014). However, organic dyes have some disadvantages, such as easy photobleaching, low quantum yield, wide emission bandwidth and narrow excitation range, while semiconductor quantum dots may contain heavy metal elements such as cadmium and selenium. The inherent toxicity, low water solubility and biological incompatibility all limit its application (Chen et al., 2020).

As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention because of the unique optical properties, good biocompatibility, green preparation process and great application potential. It may be popularized by replacing traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in the field of analysis (Lu et al., 2017; Bhati et al., 2018). Recently research show that, carbon dots can be used to detect tetracycline based on the inner filtration effect (IFE) caused by the absorption of fluorophores excited and/or emitted by absorbents in the detection system (Zhao et al., 2018). Therefore, CDs can be used as fluorescent sensors in the quantitative determination of TC. However, the quantum yield of CDs is low, and the fluorescence quenching effect of tetracycline on CDs is weak, and the decrease of fluorescence intensity is not obvious (Liu et al., 2018).

The europium ion complex has a strong red fluorescence emission peak at 617 nm and has unique photophysical properties such as large Stokes shift, high color purity and narrow emission band, which is convenient for fluorescence observation and resolution (Xu et al., 2017). In addition, the excited state lifetime of lanthanide ion complexes is in the range of microseconds to milliseconds, and the long-lived excited states can be distinguished from various short-lived noise signals (Gao et al., 2018). Based on these advantages, nanomaterials of lanthanide metal have been widely used in biological and chemical sensing for optical detection and identification of toxic substances (Bünzli, 2010). Tetracycline can form a stable complex (Eu-TC) with rare earth europium ions through its own β-diketone structure, the energy absorbed by TC from the excitation of ultraviolet radiation can be effectively transferred to Eu3+ through the "antenna effect (AE)", thus enhancing the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ (Li et al., 2018). Therefore, the integration of CDs and rare earth complex is expected to achieve high sensitive ratio fluorescence detection of TC. However, the particles of CDs are too small to be separated, while the absolute rare earth complexes have photo-thermal stability, so it is particularly necessary to find a suitable carrier of CDs and rare earth complex.

Attapulgite (Atta) is a kind of natural clay material with nanostructure, which is composed by hydrated magnesium aluminum silicate (Wang et al., 2015). Atta exists in the form of fibrous silicate clay minerals in nature, with strong adsorption performance and large specific surface area (Wang et al., 2017). Compared with other nano synthetic carriers, attapulgite is very inexpensive and easy to obtain (Zhou et al., 2016). In addition, the surface of attapulgite clay is rich in silicon hydroxyl groups and is easy to be functionalized, which makes attapulgite easy to combine with a variety of organic-inorganic fluorescent materials (Silva et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018).

In this research, a novel Atta-based ratiometric fluorescent nano-sensor was designed and assembled for the detection of tetracycline ( Scheme 1). Firstly, carboxyl-rich CDs are prepared in-situ on the surface of Atta by hydrothermal method using polyethyleneimine (PEI) modified CDs and citric acid. Secondly, Eu3+ is coordinated with carboxylic acid on the surface of CDs to prepare Atta-CDs-Eu fluorescent nano-sensor for TC detection. In the absence of TC, the nano-sensor emits blue fluorescence of CDs. With the addition of TC, the blue fluorescence of CDs is quenched, the energy transfer from TC to Eu3+ increases gradually, and the red fluorescence increases gradually. With the help of ultraviolet lamp, the nano-sensor can realize the gradual change from blue fluorescence to red fluorescence. Moreover, the fluorescent nano-sensor has good selectivity, good detection sensitivity and wide detection range. The nano-sensor has also been applied to the detection of TC in real liquid food, which indicates that the as-prepared nano-sensor in this experiment has potential practical application value. In addition, portable test strips and detection sticks based on the fluorescent nano-sensor are also developed, which provide a convenient and reliable method for the detection of TC in different liquid foods. In view of the fact that the prepared nano-sensor has a good discrimination of fluorescence color change, it can be extended to the field of logic gate devices. Through the logic gate switch designed by variables of fluorescent color, the semi-quantitative intelligent monitoring of tetracycline can be realized.

All the reagents used in this experiment were purchased and without further purification. Thiamphenicol (TAP), kanamycin sulfate (KS), ofloxacin (OFL), chloramphenicol (CAP), Oxytetracycline (OTC), 3-chloropropyltrimethoxysilane (CPTEOS, 99%), polyethyleneimine (PEI, M.W. 10000) and europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Attapulgite (Atta, Jiangsu Autobang Co., Ltd, China) was suspended in (NaPO3)6 aqueous solution, and then treated with hydrochloric acid and H2O2 (Xu et al., 2018).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) were determined by Tecnai-G2-F30 accompanied by an operating acceleration voltage of 300 kV. The surface composition of samples was performed by a Thermo Scientific ESCALab 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out on the Perkin Elmer Lambda 950 ultraviolet spectrophotometer. The nitrogen (N2) adsorption-desorption isotherm was carried out by an ASAP 2010 analyzer with nitrogen. Powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) was obtained by Rigaku-Dmax2400 X-ray diffractometer using Cu-kα radiation over the 2θ range of 5–90°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (KBr pellet method) was carried out using a Bruker-Vetx 70 infrared spectrometer within the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. The content of Eu3+ ion was determined by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) using an IRIS Advantage ER/S spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrum was measured on a Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer.

The grafting of PEI onto the surface of activated Atta was carried out according to the previous method with some modifications (Pang et al., 2011; Han et al., 2014), and the typical procedures were as follows. Firstly, the activated Atta (1.0 g) and 3.5 mL of γ-chloropropyl trimethoxysilane (CPTEOS) were dispersed by sonication in xylene (50 mL). After stirring at 80 °C for 8 h, chlopropylation-Atta (Cl-Atta) was separated by centrifuging and washed with anhydrous ethanol for five times and then dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. Secondly, all the Cl-Atta prepared in the previous step was added into 50 mL PEI aqueous solution with 10% concentration, the reaction was stirred at 90 °C for 8 h. The obtained Atta-PEI was separated by centrifuging (6000 rpm) and washed by ethanol for five times and then dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

Specifically, Atta-PEI (500 mg) and citric acid (500 mg) were ultrasonic dispersed in 15 mL deionized water and fully stirred for 1 h. The obtained solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. After the reaction kettle was naturally cooled, the solids were collected by centrifugation (6000 rpm), and then washed with anhydrous ethanol for five times to remove unreacted organic matter and free carbon quantum dots. Finally, the product was dried overnight in vacuum oven at 60 °C.

Atta-CDs (100 mg) was dispersed in anhydrous ethanol (10 mL) and stirred for 30 min, then 20 mg Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was added, and the solution was heated at 60 °C for 5 h. The final product was obtained by centrifugation (6000 rpm), and washed several times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, and then dried overnight in oven at 60 °C.

Tetracycline was dissolved in deionized water to prepare a certain concentration of tetracycline standard solution. The nano-sensor (20 μL, 1.0 mg mL−1 in deionized water) was dispersed in 2 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH = 8.0), and different amounts of tetracycline standard solution were added. The fluorescence emission spectra of the nano-sensor at different concentrations were recorded. According to the change of fluorescence intensity, the detection limit and sensitivity of tetracycline were calculated. For the detection of the actual sample, the same operation was carried out as above.

In order to test the interference of different substances on the detection of tetracycline, different aqueous solutions of metal ions (K+，Ca2+，Cu2+，Mg2+，Zn2+，Pb2+，Hg2+) and common organic nutrients (Asp, His, Glu, Lys, Cys, GSH, glucose) were prepared. At first, the emission spectrum of the nano-sensor solution prepared above was recorded under 365 nm excitation. Then 5 μM different substances to be detected were added, and the emission spectra of these detection systems containing interfering substances were recorded under 365 nm excitation. In order to test the selective detection of different antibiotics by nano-sensor, different aqueous solutions of antibiotics (Adriamycin (ADM), tigecycline (TIC), Minocycline (MC), thiamphenicol (TAP), kanamycin (KS), ofloxacin (OFL) and chloramphenicol (CAP)) were prepared and mixed with 5 μM different antibiotics, and the emission spectra of the detection system were recorded under 365 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prefabrication and detection of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The method of treating milk and honey was reported in the literature (Hu et al., 2020). The samples were purchased from local supermarkets. Firstly, 300 mg/mL trichloroacetic acid (CCl3COOH) was added into the milk with a concentration of 1% (v/v), following by the ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. The solution was then centrifuged (5 min, 8000 rpm) to remove lipids and protein and the supernatant was further filtered using a microporous membrane (0.22 µm, Lab Instrument Co., Ltd). Finally, a series of tetracycline standard solutions of different concentrations in the above supernatant were prepared. The processing method of honey sample was as follows: The honey was diluted 20 times with deionized water and purified with a microporous membrane (0.22 µm, Lab Instrument Co., Ltd). A series of tetracycline standard solutions of different concentrations in the above supernatant were prepared.

The lake water samples were taken from LongYuan Lake (JiaoZuo, China) and treated as the method described before (Hong et al., 2020). First, the lake water was centrifuged at a speed of 10,000 rpm to remove large particles of impurities. Then, the obtained supernate was carefully collected and filtered using a microporous membrane (0.22 µm, Lab Instrument Co., Ltd) to remove small particles of impurities. Finally, some standard samples with different concentrations of tetracycline were prepared with the treated lake water.

Tap water was taken directly from the faucet in the laboratory without superfluous purification and treatment. Tetracycline standard samples of different concentrations were prepared directly with tap water and set aside.

The Atta-CDs-Eu nano-sensor was dispersed in the Tris-HCl buffer (deionized water, 50 mM, pH = 8.0) and then added to the samples containing different concentrations of tetracycline (milk, honey, lake water, tap water), and the fluorescence emission spectra at 365 nm excitation of the detection system were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of test paper modified by Atta-CDs-Eu to visual detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: For paper-based TC sensing, the test paper was prepared on ordinary filter paper loaded with Atta-CDs-Eu nano sensor. Firstly, 4 mg of Atta-CDs-Eu was dispersed in 5 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH = 8.0) to prepare fluorescent nano-sensor dispersion. Secondly, the ordinary filter paper with the same thickness was cut into circular filter paper of the same size by 8 mm hole punch, then 40 pieces of circular filter paper were immersed in Atta-CDs-Eu fluorescent nano-sensor solution (0.8 mg/mL, 5 mL) for 1 h. The final filter papers with fluorescent nano-sensor were naturally dried at room temperature and used for subsequent paper-based sensing. Dripping the test solution onto the obtained filter papers, the qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis of tetracycline in the test solution can be realized with the help of ultraviolet lamp irradiation and the color capture and chromaticity analysis APP on the smart phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction of the stick-like nanosenser for visual detection of tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of fluorescent stick-like nanosenser can be divided into two steps. Firstly, the hydrophilic tape (Deli group Co., Ltd) was cut into 1.5 × 1 cm cubes, and each piece of hydrophilic tape was coated with 1 mg of Atta-CDs-Eu nanometer powder, then soaked in different concentrations of tetracycline standard solution for 5 min. After natural drying, the above square pieces were pasted on the glass rod according to the order of concentration from low to high, and then covered with hydrophobic tape (Deli Group Co., Ltd.) to prepare a series of colorimetric references for the standard concentration of tetracyclines. Then, the hydrophilic tape attached to the same amount of Atta-CDs-Eu nano-powder was pasted between the colorimetric reference bands to make a complete nano-fluorescent detection stick. In the process of detection, the stick-like nano-sensor were stirred in the actual sample for 5 min, and then different fluorescence colors were observed under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp to determine the actual concentration of tetracycline.

The FT-IR spectra of Atta, Atta-PEI, Atta-CDs and Atta-CDs-Eu were illustrated in Fig. 1a. The strong Si-OH vibration of Atta at 3551, 3414 and 1630 cm−1 may be attributed to the coordination water of Atta. The infrared absorption peak at 1030 and 791 cm−1 may be caused by the vibration of Si-O-Si skeleton. The peak at 989 cm−1 may be caused by the tensile vibration of Si-O-Mg (Xu et al., 2018). These strong infrared absorption peaks caused by the functional groups of the matrix of the nano-sensor may mask the important infrared absorption peaks of other modifiers. Obviously, the absorption peaks at 2956 and 2850 cm−1 don’t belong to Atta, so it should be caused by the stretching vibrations of C–H in the backbone of PEI modified on Atta (Massaro et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018). The peak at 1316 cm−1 is belonged to the skeletal vibration of NCOO, which is the result of the reaction of NH2/NH with the CO2 from atmospheric (Wang et al., 2009; Ma et al., 2017). The absorption peaks near 1476 and 1572 cm−1 are caused by N–H stretching vibration and -NH2 scissoring vibration (Ma et al., 2017). The new absorption peaks at 1650 and 1410 cm−1 of Atta-CDs are caused by the stretching vibration of C˭O and C–O, respectively (Zhong et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020), which indicate the existence of carboxylic acid functional groups on Atta-CDs. While after the coordination of carboxylic acid groups and Eu3+, the stretching vibrational absorption peak of carboxylic acid at 1651 cm−1 is red-shifted to 1631 cm−1, otherwise the strong absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 is caused by nitrate coordinated with Eu3+ (Chen et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018), which confirm the coordination of carboxylic acid and europium on the Atta-CDs nano-sensor.

In order to understand the crystal structures of nano-fluorescent materials, X-ray diffraction detection was carried out. As shown in Fig. 1b, the peaks at 8.50°, 13.74°, 16.40°, 19.88°, 20.85°, 21.45°, 26.75°, 27.50°, 34.45° and 35.20° are corresponded to (110), (200), (130), (040), (121), (240), (231), (400), (440) and (161) crystal planes of Atta (JCPDS No.21-0958), respectively (Wang et al., 2020), which indicate that the Atta carrier of fluorescent materials maintained its own crystal structure throughout the experiment, and the modification of Atta by CPTEOS and PEI occur on its exterior surface, grooves or channel entrance, rather than in the internal interlayer of Atta (Zhang et al., 2015). However, the XRD spectra of Atta-CDs and Atta-CDs-Eu show a broad peak centered at 21.15°, which can be attributed to the highly disordered and amorphous carbon structure of carbon quantum dots on the surface of Atta (Gunjal et al., 2019).

The morphologies of Atta and Atta-CDs-Eu nanomaterials were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The Atta displays rod-shaped morphology, with the diameter of 50 nm and the length is about 100–300 nm (Fig. 1c and d). However, after Atta is modified with carbon quantum dots and further coordinated with europium, there are many small particles about 3 nm in size on the surface of Atta, which indicate that the CDs are successfully modified on the surface of Atta. The chemical composition of Atta-CDs-Eu was measured by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS), the appearance of N, Si, O and especially Eu elements proves the successful modification of Eu on Atta-CDs (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the content of Eu was determined to 2.5% by ICP-OES, which also indicate that Eu has been entered Atta-CDs.

To further determine the chemical component of the Atta-CDs-Eu, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed ( Fig. 2). From the full scan XPS spectrum of Atta-CDs-Eu (Fig. 2a), it can be seen that the peaks of C, N, O and Eu appear at 285 eV (C 1s), 399.8 eV (N 1s), 531.5 eV (O 1s), 1134.5 eV (Eu 3d5/2) and 1164.5 eV (Eu 3d3/2), respectively. Furthermore, in Atta-CDs-Eu, representative peaks for the Si 2s, Si 2p, Al 2p, Mg 2p and O 2s were also observed, attributing the existence of Atta. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b), the four main peaks at 284.06, 285.2, 286.0, and 287.6 eV can be assigned to carbon in the form of C–C, C–N, C–O, and C˭O, respectively (Li et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2014). In the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S2), the five peaks at 398.48, 399.15, 399.96, 401.12 and 406.15 eV can be assigned to nitrogen species of in the forms of C–N–C, N–H, N–[C]3, C–NH2 and NO3 -, respectively (Zhang et al., 2014; Sharma and Basu, 2020). The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) indicates three fitted peaks at 530.6, 531.45 and 532.50 eV, attributing to the C˭O, C–O and Si–O groups, respectively (Qi et al., 2019; Sharma and Basu, 2020). Concerning europium (Fig. 2d), there are four peaks due to multiple splitting of the spin-orbit j = 5/2 and 3/2 components. The peaks at 1134.62 and 1164.43 eV belong to Eu3+ 3d5/2 and Eu3+ 3d3/2, respectively (Shurygin et al., 2019), while 1124.4 and 1154.30 eV indicate the Eu2+ 3d5/2 and Eu2+ 3d3/2 energy levels. The j-j energy separation of energy difference between two Eu3+ is near 29.8 eV, which is consistent with the data of oxygen coordinated Eu3+ (Armelao et al., 2008; Trandafilovic et al., 2017). These results proved that the in-situ synthesis of CDs and functional modification of Eu-complex are successfully completed on the surface of Atta.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of ratiometric Atta-CDs-Eu nan-sensor of TC
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated in the Fig. S3, the emission peaks of CDs have no remarkable shift with the excitation changed from 340 to 390 nm, which further verifies that the CDs are limited onto the surface of Atta (Massaro et al., 2018). The maximum emission wavelengths of CDs are located at 445 nm under 365 nm excitation. Therefore, the fluorescence response of Atta-CDs-Eu to TC under 365 nm excitation was measured to illustrate the sensing mechanism of the nano-sensor ( Fig. 3a). When excited with 365 nm without TC, Atta-CDs-Eu only shows the fluorescence of CDs (445 nm), while Eu complexes basically don’t emit (Fig. 3a). Compared with the traditional monochromatic fluorescence sensor, the ratio sensor can save the calibration curve needed for measuring instrument and concentration correction, and improve the sensitivity of detection. With the increase of the concentration of tetracycline, the characteristic emission from Eu (Ⅲ) ion (5D0) to the ground state (7FJ, J = 1, 2, 3, 4) appears regular strong emission lines at 593, 617, 652 and 695 nm, among which 5D0/7F2 has the most prominent emission peak (the dashed curve in Fig. 3a, the energy transfer mechanism diagram of Atta-CDs-Eu with the increasing of TC is shown in Fig. 3b. Due to the antenna effect (AE), TC as a sensitizer, contains β-diketone structure, which can coordinate with Eu3+ ions and the energy absorbed by tetracycline from the excitation of ultraviolet radiation can effectively transfer to Eu3+. Furthermore, TC can completely replace H2O in Eu3+ center. The competition between tetracycline and the coordination water molecules on rare earth europium will change the fluorescence of the nano-sensor. The number of coordination water molecules can be determined by calculating the change of fluorescence lifetime of rare earth chelates in H2O and D2O systems before and after the introduction of tetracycline (Fig. 3c).

According to the equation established by Supkowski and Horrocks (2002), the coordination "H2O" number (q) in different system was calculated ( Table 1), and the q value in the nano-sensor was given by
(1)
q = 1.1 [
τ

O
− 1
−

− 0.31 ]
Where

and
τ

2
O
are the lifetimes of Eu(III) in various complexes in H2O and D2O buffer, respectively. The q value decreases from 6.7 before joining TC to 2.7 after joining TC. The decrease of q value shows that tetracycline molecules have more affinity for europium ions than water molecules.

When the water molecule is coordinated with europium ion, the excited state of europium ion would vibrate with the high frequency ·OH oscillator, which would cause the loss of non-radiation energy. Obviously, the energy release from the excited state back to the ground state in the form of fluorescence radiation will be weakened, that is, the fluorescence intensity of europium ion is relatively low. These reasons lead to the characteristic emission of Eu(III) ions, indicating that Atta-CDs-Eu nanomaterials are suitable for the ratio detection of TC. And under the ultraviolet lamp, the color change of the emission is recognized by the naked eye, providing the basis for visual detection and field sensing. This is consistent with the information provided by the absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. S4, compared with the absence of tetracycline, the absorption peak of Atta-CDs-Eu with TC has a significant red shift, and the absorbance is also significantly increased, indicating the europium can coordinate with TC and sensitize the luminescence of Eu3+.

In order to verify the tetracycline-induced fluorescence quenching of carbon quantum dots is originated from IFE, the UV spectra (Fig. S3) of related samples were measured. It is obvious that the introduction of TC does not lead to the emergence of a new absorption peak, which proves that the substance in the nano-sensor has not changed. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of tetracycline. It is speculated that the internal filtering effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) in polychromatic fluorescence system may lead to fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (Yan et al., 2020). Therefore, the effect of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) can be eliminated by investigating whether the fluorescence lifetime of the system changes before and after the introduction of TC. It can be seen from Fig. 3d and Table S1 that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs changes slightly after the addition of TC, indicating the fluorescence quenched by CDs is mainly caused by IFE (Yan et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for ratiometric fluorescent assay of TC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effect of pH value on the working condition of the nano-sensor, Atta-CDs-Eu was placed in a series of tris-HCl buffer aqueous solutions with different pH values to study the changes of fluorescence intensity of CDs and Eu3+ in the presence of TC (Fig. S5a). The fluorescence intensity of CDs at 445 nm is stronger in the neutral condition, which may be related to the surface functional groups of CDs. However, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 617 nm changes more obviously with the pH value. Under acidic conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ is weak, and the increase of pH value of the system will lead to the deprotonation of tetracycline molecules and the formation of Eu-TC complex, which eventually leads to a significant increase in the fluorescence intensity of the system (Jia et al., 2019). However, when the pH value of the detection system is greater than 9.0, the fluorescence intensity of the detection system decreases sharply, which may be due to the precipitation of europium hydroxide. According to the change of pH value, the intensity changes of red fluorescence and blue fluorescence are plotted (Tan and Chen, 2012). Comprehensively, the best working condition of nano-sensor is pH = 8.

In addition, in order to explore the best response time of the nano-sensor after the addition of tetracycline, the change of fluorescence intensity of the nano-sensor after the addition of tetracycline was tested and recorded in a certain time interval. The experimental results listed in Fig. S5b show that the fluorescence intensity of the nano-sensor can be enhanced by more than 90% after the addition of tetracycline for 5 min, which indicate that the nano-sensor has the potential for rapid detection of tetracycline.

After optimizing the best experimental parameters, the response performance of Atta-CDs-Eu polychromatic fluorescent nano-sensor to TC was tested. As shown in Fig. 4a, when the concentration of TC increases from 0 to 20 μM, the blue fluorescence of the sensing system at 445 nm is gradually weakened, while the red fluorescence at 617 nm is gradually enhanced. The linear equation of correlation can be expressed as (I617/I445 = 0.193[TC]−0.0116) (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) are determined as 8.7 nM and 28.4 nM through 3σ/slope and 10 σ/slope analysis (Wei et al., 2017), respectively, which are much lower than the maximum allowable concentration of tetracycline in milk set by the European Union (225 nM) (Kurittu et al., 2000).

The change of fluorescence color of the detection system can be further revealed by CIE chromaticity coordinates. It can be seen from Fig. 4c that when the concentration of tetracycline increases gradually, CIE coordinates have a continuous change from blue (0.1647,0.1233) to red (0.3898,0.2333). More importantly, the fluorescence color of the actual test sample corresponding to Fig. 4c also shows a transition from blue to red, indicating that the nano-sensor can realize the multi-color visual detection of tetracycline (Fig. 4d). In addition, this method is also compared with the method reported in the literature for the determination of tetracycline residues by high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MSMS) and other methods. The results are summarized in Table S2. Compared with HPLC-MSMS method, our method could achieve a wider linearity range from 0.025 to 20 μM, corresponding to 11.1–8880 μg L-1, which is superior to HPLC-MSMS method. The LOD is 8.7 nM, corresponding to 3.86 μg L-1, which is comparable with HPLC-MSMS method. However, the method reported here can achieve rapid visual quantitative, qualitative and semi-quantitative detection of tetracycline, while the previously reported method is much more complex than the current visual fluorescence method.

As a comparison, Atta-CDs as single signal fluorescent nano-sensor was also used to TC detection. As shown in Fig. 5a and b, the fluorescence intensity of Atta-CDs decline with the increase of TC and the Atta-CDs nano-sensor displays a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) with the LOD of 37.5 nM in the range of 0.1–20 μM. Compared with the Atta-CDs-Eu ratiometric fluorescent nano-sensor, the Atta-CDs nano-sensor has lower sensitivity. More obviously, the Atta-CDs nano-sensor could only display the brightness change of blue fluorescence for the detection of TC (Fig. 5c). Under a 365 nm Ultraviolet lamp, the concentration of tetracycline labeled with fluorescent color change is difficult to distinguish by naked eyes (Fig. 5d).

In order to further verify the excellent performance of the nano-sensor, the TC detection performance of absolute europium complex (Cit-Eu) was also compared. As shown in Fig. 6a, the detection ability of Cit-Eu to tetracycline is unconspicuous. In addition, the detection limit is about 63 nM, and the linear range is between 0 and 4 μM (Fig. 6b). At the same time, the change of CIE color coordinates is not obvious (Fig. 6c). Under the UV lamp, it can only show the change from colorless to red fluorescence (Fig. 6d). These results show that the ratio fluorescence method based on Atta-CDs-Eu has higher sensitivity and accuracy than the single fluorescence method, and has more potential for naked eye vision analysis of tetracycline sensor. Meanwhile, as displayed in Fig. S6, no distinct variation in fluorescence intensity was found after the addition of interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from Table 2 that when the spiked concentration of TC is 0.5, 2 and 5 μM, the recovery rates are 96.30–104.42% for all the samples, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) are between 1.13% and 3.68%. Moreover, the LOD and LOQ in four representative type of real samples are presented, as shown in Table S3, good linearity can be obtained for real samples. The LODs and LOQs are found to be 23.84–27.07 nM and 79.47–90.23 nM for different real samples. These also indicated the nano-sensor prepared here has good application in the detection of TC in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted sensing platform for visual detection of TC by functionalized test paper
ce:section.ce:para: In order to realize the on-site detection of TC in the actual sample, the semi-quantitative sensing of TC was carried out by using the color pick-up and data analysis program in the smartphone (Han et al., 2020). Fig. 7 illustrates the sensing process based on smartphones. First, under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, the images of Atta-CDs-Eu nano-sensor to different concentrations of tetracycline are taken by smartphone. As the fluorescence color of the nano-sensor changes gradually from blue to red in the process of TC detection, the tested polychromatic light signals of the sample can be converted into the RGB value under the two channels of (R) and blue (B) by using the chromaticity pick-up APP in the mobile phone. The intelligent semi-quantitative analysis of TC can be realized by investigating the relationship between the fluorescence values and the concentration of TC in the above two channels. There is a good linear correlation between the correlation RGB value and TC between 0.1 and 1.75 μM (R2 = 0.99), and the correlation linear equation is: R/B = 0.502[TC] + 0.223, which indicates the above intelligent on-site semi-quantitative detection platform for TC has great application potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of tetracycline by functional detection rods of nano-fluorescent materials
ce:section.ce:para: In order to quickly detect the concentration of tetracycline in real liquid food samples, a special stick-like detection rod was assembled and used for naked eye colorimetry under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp by using colorless gel and transparent glass rod (which can eliminate the influence of background fluorescence). Specifically, the power of the fluorescent nano-sensor is adhered to the glass rod with hydrophilic tape, and then these samples are immersed in standard tetracycline samples of different concentrations for 5 min and then taken out ( Fig. 8). After drying, the fluorescence glue was encapsulated with hydrophobic tape to isolate the effect of tetracycline in the sample, which will be used as standard colorimetric sticks. Then the same hydrophilic tape with the fluorescent nano-sensor powder is affixed around the standard colorimetric sticks. Fig. 8a and b are photos of the nano-sensor itself under natural light and ultraviolet lamp. Under UV light, the hydrophobic part clearly shows the fluorescent color of the known concentration of TC standard. When the nano-sensor is gently stirred in the milk sample for a moment (Fig. 8c), it can be seen from Fig. 8d and e that the fluorescence color of the hydrophobic part of the nano-sensor does not change with the help of ultraviolet lamp, while the hydrophilic part shows a certain fluorescent color. By comparing the color of the nano-sensor with the standard concentration fluorescence color after the addition of TC, the approximate concentration of TC in the system can be determined clearly and quickly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of fluorescent sensor for tetracycline detection in logic gate circuit
ce:section.ce:para: According to the discoloration of Atta-CDs-Eu fluorescent nano-sensor caused by TC, nano-sensor was applied to the logic gate circuit ( Fig. 9a) for semi-quantitative detection of TC concentration. The fluorescence color is used as the input and the concentration of tetracycline as the output. The normalized luminous intensity excited in 445 nm (I-blue, input 2) and 617 nm (I-red, input 1) is selected as the double input signal (Fig. 9b). When the detection system emits red fluorescence, the input signal "1" of input1 is defined. When the detection system does not emit red fluorescence, the input signal "0" of input 1 is defined. Similarly, when the detection system emits blue fluorescence, the input signal "1" of input 2 is defined. When the detection system does not emit blue fluorescence, the input signal "2" of input 2 is defined. Combined with the actual detection situation, the dose of tetracycline of 2 μM and 5 μM is taken as the threshold. When the amount of tetracycline is less than 2 μM, the detection system is defined to emit no red fluorescence but only blue fluorescence. When the amount of tetracycline is higher than 5 μM, the detection system is defined to emit no blue fluorescence but only red fluorescence. Between those concentration, it is defined as emitting both blue and red fluorescence. Therefore, the input (0,1) indicates that the concentration of tetracycline is at a low level or not (the output is (1,0,0)), the input (1,1) indicates that the concentration of tetracycline is in the middle concentration of 2–5 μM (the output is 0,1,0), and the input (1,0) indicates that the concentration of tetracycline is at a high concentration greater than 5 μM (the output is (0,0,1)) ( Table 3).

In brief, a polychromatic fluorescence nano-sensor based on Atta is reported in this paper, which can realize the ultra-sensitive and wide range detection of tetracycline in the concentration range from 50 nM to 20 μM. The low detection limit (8.7 nM) coupled with good accuracy, sensitivity and specificity meets the requirements for the quality and safety of control and detection of tetracycline in food. The LODs and LOQs are found to be 23.84–27.07 nM and 79.47–90.23 nM for different real samples. Compared with HPLC-MSMS method, our method could achieve a wider linearity range from 0.025 to 20 μM, corresponding to 11.1–8880 μg L-1, which is superior to HPLC-MSMS method. Interestingly, through the chromaticity analysis application on the smartphone, tetracycline can be intelligently identified by a visual nano-sensor based on the test strip. In addition, the Atta-CDs-Eu material is also made into a stick-like nano-sensor to realize the real-time detection of tetracycline residues in a variety of liquid foods. Finally, based on the fluorescence color change of the detection system caused by the change of TC concentration, a logic gate switch is designed, which provides the possibility for the application of nano-sensors in the field of smart devices. After further optimization, the strategy also has a broad application prospect in the field detection of other antibiotics.

Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Rujie Chen: Visualization, Investigation, Software. Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Lina Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiangzhen Chen: Formal analysis, Software. Ning Bi: Software, Validation. Jian Gou: Formal analysis, Validation. Tongqian Zhao: Supervision, Methodology.
